architecture.summary: GradSAE is a training-free method that identifies causally influential latent features in Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) by incorporating output-side gradient information. Given a pre-trained SAE decomposing hidden representations into sparse latent activations, GradSAE approximates the causal influence of each latent on the model output via first-order Taylor expansion of the output logits with respect to latent activations. The key insight is that latents with high input-side activation do not necessarily have high causal influence on outputs; instead, influence is measured by the magnitude of the latent activation multiplied by the gradient of the output logit with respect to that activation. The method ranks latents by their average influence scores across the input sequence and selects top-K influential latents for downstream tasks like model steering.

biblio.title: Beyond Input Activations: Identifying Influential Latents by Gradient Sparse Autoencoders

claims.statement: Not all activated latents contribute equally to the construction of the model's output
Only latents with high causal influence are effective for model steering
Masking GradSAE's TopK latents at K=50% results in F1 score dropping from 94.79% to 43.33%
GradSAE TopK latent masking causes larger performance degradation (80.45% EM at K=1) compared to Baseline method (97.04% EM at K=1)
Masking BottomK latents maintains near-perfect performance across all K values, with F1 scores around 99% for both Baseline and GradSAE
In local steering, GradSAE achieves F1 score of 10.21% at K=10, compared to Baseline's 6.69%, demonstrating more effective steering of model outputs toward a different question
Only 18.06% of latents in Baseline's TopK set (K=50%) overlap with GradSAE's TopK set, indicating that high input-side activation does not reliably indicate causal influence on output
GradSAE generalizes across different SAEs and instruction-tuned LLMs; experiments on LLaMA 3 8B Instruct show consistent superiority of GradSAE over Baseline in identifying influential latents
